IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): MinIE addresses a practical and important weakness in OIE—overly specific and factually noisy extractions—by introducing factuality-aware minimization and configurable modes, and demonstrates competitive or improved precision/recall while producing much shorter extractions. From an experimentalist perspective this is a useful, implementable advance (code available, built on ClausIE) that practitioners can adopt and that should change how extractions are post-processed for downstream IE tasks. The work also opens clear follow-on research (better parsers, richer factuality annotation, integration with neural OIE) while being limited by dependency-parsing errors and evaluation on relatively small manual samples. | B(3): MinIE tackles a real and practical problem in OIE—producing compact, fact-focused extractions with explicit polarity/modality/attribution/quantity annotations—which is valuable for downstream KB construction and NLP pipelines. Built on ClausIE and shown to yield competitive precision/recall while producing much shorter extractions (with configurable minimization modes), the work is a useful engineering advance that improves usability of OIE outputs. However, it is largely an applied refinement rather than a paradigm shift: dependency-parsing errors and reliance on heuristics limit generality, so its impact is moderate and likely to spur incremental follow-ups rather than fundamentally change the field.

reviewer_a.justification: MinIE addresses a practical and important weakness in OIE—overly specific and factually noisy extractions—by introducing factuality-aware minimization and configurable modes, and demonstrates competitive or improved precision/recall while producing much shorter extractions. From an experimentalist perspective this is a useful, implementable advance (code available, built on ClausIE) that practitioners can adopt and that should change how extractions are post-processed for downstream IE tasks. The work also opens clear follow-on research (better parsers, richer factuality annotation, integration with neural OIE) while being limited by dependency-parsing errors and evaluation on relatively small manual samples.

reviewer_b.justification: MinIE tackles a real and practical problem in OIE—producing compact, fact-focused extractions with explicit polarity/modality/attribution/quantity annotations—which is valuable for downstream KB construction and NLP pipelines. Built on ClausIE and shown to yield competitive precision/recall while producing much shorter extractions (with configurable minimization modes), the work is a useful engineering advance that improves usability of OIE outputs. However, it is largely an applied refinement rather than a paradigm shift: dependency-parsing errors and reliance on heuristics limit generality, so its impact is moderate and likely to spur incremental follow-ups rather than fundamentally change the field.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): MinIE proposes a clear new perspective for OIE by explicitly minimizing extractions and representing factuality (polarity, modality, attribution, quantities) as annotations rather than only producing larger clause-based triples. Although implemented on top of ClausIE and evaluated against existing OIE systems (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE), the introduced minimization modes (Safe, Frequency, Collocations, Aggressive) and the annotation scheme constitute a non-trivial extension beyond prior work. From an experimentalist standpoint this would likely inform practitioners and experts, since it changes the output representation and trade-offs (much shorter extractions with competitive precision/recall) rather than merely tuning prior pipelines. | B(4): MinIE introduces a clear new perspective for OIE—systematic minimization of clause-based extractions combined with annotated factuality (polarity, modality, attribution, quantities)—which is not standard in prior systems. While it builds on ClausIE and uses known components (dependency parsing, clause decomposition), the configurable minimization modes and explicit factual annotations constitute a non-trivial methodological extension that is clearly differentiated from prior OIE work (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE). An expert would find the focus on shortening extractions while preserving factuality informative and a notable advance beyond incremental system tuning, even if the core pipeline leverages existing techniques.

Although implemented on top of ClausIE and evaluated against existing OIE systems (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE), the introduced minimization modes (Safe, Frequency, Collocations, Aggressive) and the annotation scheme constitute a non-trivial extension beyond prior work. From an experimentalist standpoint this would likely inform practitioners and experts, since it changes the output representation and trade-offs (much shorter extractions with competitive precision/recall) rather than merely tuning prior pipelines.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by empirical results showing competitive precision/recall and substantially shorter extractions, and the method is implemented and compared against sensible baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on NYT/Wiki samples. However, important methodological details that affect reproducibility and strength of evidence are missing: hyperparameters are not reported, there is no mention of statistical significance testing or inter-annotator agreement for precision judgments, and only limited ablation/sensitivity analyses of the minimization modes are described. These are minor but material issues that do not overturn the core technical contribution, so I rate it a 4. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: MinIE is implemented on ClausIE, evaluated against reasonable OIE baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on multiple datasets, and reported metrics (precision, attribution precision, recall, extraction length) substantiate the benefits of minimization. There are no formal mathematical claims to verify, and the experimental methodology is largely appropriate, but the summary lacks details on statistical testing and hyperparameter reporting, and known error sources (dependency parsing, NER, collocation coverage) expose practical limitations. Overall the technical approach and results are convincing though some experimental rigor and robustness analysis are missing, so minor issues remain.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by empirical results showing competitive precision/recall and substantially shorter extractions, and the method is implemented and compared against sensible baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on NYT/Wiki samples. However, important methodological details that affect reproducibility and strength of evidence are missing: hyperparameters are not reported, there is no mention of statistical significance testing or inter-annotator agreement for precision judgments, and only limited ablation/sensitivity analyses of the minimization modes are described. These are minor but material issues that do not overturn the core technical contribution, so I rate it a 4.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: MinIE is implemented on ClausIE, evaluated against reasonable OIE baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on multiple datasets, and reported metrics (precision, attribution precision, recall, extraction length) substantiate the benefits of minimization. There are no formal mathematical claims to verify, and the experimental methodology is largely appropriate, but the summary lacks details on statistical testing and hyperparameter reporting, and known error sources (dependency parsing, NER, collocation coverage) expose practical limitations. Overall the technical approach and results are convincing though some experimental rigor and robustness analysis are missing, so minor issues remain.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares MinIE against established OIE systems (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on the same corpora (NYT samples and Wikipedia) using common metrics (precision, recall, attribution precision, extraction length), and provides code which aids reproducibility. Comparisons appear broadly fair, and the paper discusses limitations and error sources that explain when extractions fail. However, the paper does not report hyperparameter settings or statistical significance, and from the summary it is unclear whether the very latest neural OIE baselines or more extensive ablations were included, so the experimental comparison has minor omissions. | B(5): The paper compares MinIE against appropriate, established OIE baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on the same NYT and Wikipedia samples and reports comparable metrics (precision, recall, attribution precision, and extraction length), which supports fair evaluation. The authors explicitly discuss when MinIE is competitive or superior and analyze error sources (dependency parsing, NER, collocations) and the distinctions introduced by their minimization modes, and the summary notes thorough related-work coverage—together indicating comprehensive and fair comparisons with relevant prior work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares MinIE against established OIE systems (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on the same corpora (NYT samples and Wikipedia) using common metrics (precision, recall, attribution precision, extraction length), and provides code which aids reproducibility. Comparisons appear broadly fair, and the paper discusses limitations and error sources that explain when extractions fail. However, the paper does not report hyperparameter settings or statistical significance, and from the summary it is unclear whether the very latest neural OIE baselines or more extensive ablations were included, so the experimental comparison has minor omissions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares MinIE against appropriate, established OIE baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on the same NYT and Wikipedia samples and reports comparable metrics (precision, recall, attribution precision, and extraction length), which supports fair evaluation. The authors explicitly discuss when MinIE is competitive or superior and analyze error sources (dependency parsing, NER, collocations) and the distinctions introduced by their minimization modes, and the summary notes thorough related-work coverage—together indicating comprehensive and fair comparisons with relevant prior work.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents solid experimental comparisons against reasonable baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on multiple datasets and reports multiple relevant metrics (factual and attribution precision, recall, extraction length), and it provides code and discusses concrete failure modes (parsing, NER, collocations). However, sensitivity analyses and hyperparameter details are missing and there is limited reporting of statistical significance; while configurable minimization modes act like an ablation, more systematic ablation/sensitivity studies (e.g., per-component impact, parser/NER noise) would strengthen the claims. Overall the empirical evaluation is strong and reproducible in principle, but has minor gaps in analysis and experimental rigor. | B(4): The paper provides solid experimental comparisons to relevant OIE baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) across multiple corpora (NYT-10k, NYT, Wiki) and reports both factual and attribution precision plus extraction length statistics, supporting the core claims about more concise, annotated extractions. It lacks formal ablation/sensitivity analyses of the different minimization modes and does not report hyperparameters, which limits assessment of robustness and exact reproducibility despite code availability. Related work appears thoroughly covered and the authors explicitly discuss key failure modes (parsing, NER, collocation handling). Overall the empirical evaluation is strong but missing some deeper analyses and reproducibility details.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents solid experimental comparisons against reasonable baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on multiple datasets and reports multiple relevant metrics (factual and attribution precision, recall, extraction length), and it provides code and discusses concrete failure modes (parsing, NER, collocations). However, sensitivity analyses and hyperparameter details are missing and there is limited reporting of statistical significance; while configurable minimization modes act like an ablation, more systematic ablation/sensitivity studies (e.g., per-component impact, parser/NER noise) would strengthen the claims. Overall the empirical evaluation is strong and reproducible in principle, but has minor gaps in analysis and experimental rigor.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid experimental comparisons to relevant OIE baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) across multiple corpora (NYT-10k, NYT, Wiki) and reports both factual and attribution precision plus extraction length statistics, supporting the core claims about more concise, annotated extractions. It lacks formal ablation/sensitivity analyses of the different minimization modes and does not report hyperparameters, which limits assessment of robustness and exact reproducibility despite code availability. Related work appears thoroughly covered and the authors explicitly discuss key failure modes (parsing, NER, collocation handling). Overall the empirical evaluation is strong but missing some deeper analyses and reproducibility details.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This work addresses a core NLP problem (Open IE) and evaluates against established baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on standard corpora (NYT, Wiki) with relevant metrics (precision, recall, factual/attribution precision, extraction length), so the topic and audience fit a top NLP venue. The paper provides code and thorough related work coverage, strengthening reproducibility and community relevance. Minor concerns for a top-tier submission are the lack of reported hyperparameters and limited experimental details (e.g., statistical significance, annotation/IAA details) that I expect for full evaluation rigor, but these do not undermine overall topical fit. | B(5): This paper addresses a clear, well-scoped problem in Open Information Extraction—producing minimal, annotated extractions—which is directly relevant to top NLP venues concerned with IE and semantic representation. The methodology (clause-based input from ClausIE plus linguistically motivated minimization of polarity/modality/attribution/quantities), the use of standard corpora (NYT, Wikipedia), and evaluation on precision/attribution metrics align with community expectations. The work advances practical utility (shorter, annotated triples) and provides code, making it useful to both researchers and practitioners in the venue’s audience. Overall the topic, methods, and evaluation are an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This work addresses a core NLP problem (Open IE) and evaluates against established baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on standard corpora (NYT, Wiki) with relevant metrics (precision, recall, factual/attribution precision, extraction length), so the topic and audience fit a top NLP venue. The paper provides code and thorough related work coverage, strengthening reproducibility and community relevance. Minor concerns for a top-tier submission are the lack of reported hyperparameters and limited experimental details (e.g., statistical significance, annotation/IAA details) that I expect for full evaluation rigor, but these do not undermine overall topical fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a clear, well-scoped problem in Open Information Extraction—producing minimal, annotated extractions—which is directly relevant to top NLP venues concerned with IE and semantic representation. The work advances practical utility (shorter, annotated triples) and provides code, making it useful to both researchers and practitioners in the venue’s audience. Overall the topic, methods, and evaluation are an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a logical flow from motivation through method and experiments, and its main contributions (MinIE design, factuality annotations, and minimization modes) are stated early and maintained throughout. Notation and terminology are consistent and the writing quality is good, with effective figures/tables/examples that clarify how MinIE extends ClausIE and the different minimization modes. A few presentation gaps remain for reproducibility-oriented readers (e.g., omission of hyperparameter details and some implementation nuances), but overall the exposition is clear and readable. | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions (MinIE’s minimized, annotated extractions and modes) early and clearly. Notation and definitions appear consistent, and writing quality is reported as good with thorough related-work coverage; figures/tables/examples (e.g., minimization modes, error sources) effectively illustrate the ideas. Minor presentation issues remain — e.g., lack of reported hyperparameters and some dependency on external parsing/NER errors are noted but could be more explicitly explained — so it is very good but not flawless.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a logical flow from motivation through method and experiments, and its main contributions (MinIE design, factuality annotations, and minimization modes) are stated early and maintained throughout. Notation and terminology are consistent and the writing quality is good, with effective figures/tables/examples that clarify how MinIE extends ClausIE and the different minimization modes. A few presentation gaps remain for reproducibility-oriented readers (e.g., omission of hyperparameter details and some implementation nuances), but overall the exposition is clear and readable.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions (MinIE’s minimized, annotated extractions and modes) early and clearly. Notation and definitions appear consistent, and writing quality is reported as good with thorough related-work coverage; figures/tables/examples (e.g., minimization modes, error sources) effectively illustrate the ideas. Minor presentation issues remain — e.g., lack of reported hyperparameters and some dependency on external parsing/NER errors are noted but could be more explicitly explained — so it is very good but not flawless.

main_claims: MinIE produces minimized, annotated open information extraction (OIE) extractions by removing overly-specific parts and representing factuality aspects as annotations.
MinIE rewrites OIE extractions by identifying and removing polarity, modality, attribution, and quantities and provides multiple minimization modes (Safe, Frequency, Collocations, Aggressive).
MinIE is implemented on top of ClausIE and yields competitive or higher precision and recall than prior systems while producing much shorter extractions.
_summary.methodology: MinIE builds on ClausIE to obtain clause-based input extractions, enriches relations from constituents, performs basic minimization by detecting/removing polarity, modality, attribution, and quantities, and then applies configurable minimization modes (MinIE-S, -F, -C, -A) to produce minimized extractions with annotations.
datasets: NYT-10k (random sample of 10,000 sentences from the New York Times Annotated Corpus)
NYT (random sample of 200 sentences from NYT)
Wiki (random sample of 200 sentences from Wikipedia)
metrics: attribution precision (fraction of correct triples with correct attributions)
mean word count per extraction (µ) and standard deviation (σ)
limitations: Errors in dependency parsing transfer to MinIE and are a main source of errors.
Dropping adjectives can break collocations when collocations are not present in the dictionary.
Named-entity recognition errors can cause incorrect extractions (e.g., misrecognized entity boundaries).

reviewer_a.name: Experimentalist Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML/NLP engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating information extraction systems and reproducible NLP toolchains in industry and academia.
traits: insists on rigorous experimental design: clear baselines, statistical significance, and reproducible code/data
looks for comprehensive ablation studies and sensitivity analyses for algorithmic components and hyperparameters
evaluates evaluation: checks metrics, annotation quality, inter-annotator agreement, and dataset biases
values implementation details and runtime/complexity analysis to assess practicality and scalability
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on the empirical claims: verifies whether experiments, comparisons, and analyses convincingly support the paper's conclusions and whether the work can be reproduced and used in practice.

reviewer_b.name: OIE/IE Domain Expert
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher specializing in Information Extraction and semantic representation with long-standing involvement in OpenIE/OIE, knowledge base construction, and linguistic annotation schemes.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior OIE and clause-based extraction work and how contributions advance the state of the art
values clear linguistic motivation, coverage of phenomena (polarity/modality/attribution/quantities), and the usefulness of annotations for downstream tasks
considers broader impact: usability of outputs, integration with KBs, and whether the approach addresses real needs in the community
notices alignment between proposed representations and existing semantic/linguistic frameworks and potential limitations in generality
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's conceptual contribution and significance: judges how the method fits into the research landscape, its likely impact, and whether it solves meaningful problems beyond incremental engineering.
